title: On-chip levitation of ferromagnetic microparticles
authors: Martijn Janse, M. Luisa Mattana, Julian van Doorn, Eli van der Bent, Richard Wagner, Robert Smit, Bas Hensen
abstract: Levitation of microscopic objects in vacuum combines exceptional environmental isolation with precise control of their dynamics, pushing the limits of sensing and macroscopic quantum physics. In particular, magnetic levitation allows a large range of particle sizes, while avoiding detrimental effects from high-intensity optical trapping beams and electric field noise. However, existing diamagnetic and Meissner levitation approaches are typically constrained by low mechanical eigenfrequencies, limited integrability with other systems due to bulky coils or magnets, and, for Meissner levitation, the need for cryogenic operation. Here, we demonstrate a room-temperature on-chip magnetic levitation platform capable of stably levitating a nanogram (6.5 micrometer radius) ferromagnetic microsphere. The platform is scalable and tunable, and supports librational modes with eigenfrequencies exceeding 10 kHz. Further miniaturization and coupling to solid-state spin qubits could enable cooling to the quantum ground state. Beyond quantum experiments, this architecture enables integrated precision sensing and studies of isolated ferromagnet thermodynamics.
summary: The paper demonstrates a room-temperature, on-chip magnetic trap capable of levitating ferromagnetic microparticles. This platform overcomes the limitations of previous levitation methods and offers a scalable architecture for high-frequency mechanical sensing and potential coupling to quantum spin systems.
main_problem: The need for a scalable, integrable, and room-temperature platform for levitating micro-to-nanoscale objects in vacuum while avoiding the decoherence and mass limits of optical and electrical traps.
main_result: Demonstration of an on-chip magnetic Paul trap that stably levitates a nanogram-scale ferromagnetic microsphere at room temperature with high-frequency librational modes.
method: The researchers used an on-chip platform with gold current loops to create a rotating magnetic field, detecting particle motion via back-scattered laser light and analyzing the power spectral density.
model_or_system: An on-chip magnetic Paul trap featuring gold concentric current loops on a silicon/glass chip, levitating a NdFeB ferromagnetic microsphere in a vacuum environment.
key_observables: Translational and librational eigenfrequencies, damping rates, micromotion sidebands, and the temperature dependence of magnetization.
important_parameters: Nanogram mass scale, 6.5 micrometer particle radius, librational frequencies exceeding 10 kHz, and the sideband-resolved regime.
main_assumptions: Assumes the free-molecular regime for gas damping and uses a steady-state energy balance model for thermal dynamics.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the trap design and simulated potential; Figure 2 displays the experimental setup and power spectral density; Figure 5 illustrates the prospects for spin-mechanical coupling with NV centers; Figure 9 shows finite-element simulations of eddy current damping.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the motivation for magnetic levitation, describes the on-chip trap design and experimental setup, presents the characterization of mechanical modes and thermal dynamics, analyzes dissipation mechanisms, and concludes with prospects for quantum spin-mechanical coupling.
why_interesting: It presents a scalable platform for macroscopic quantum mechanics and provides a clear pathway for coupling mechanical motion to solid-state spin qubits for ground-state cooling and quantum sensing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Design and simulation of an on-chip magnetic Paul trap for ferromagnetic micro-particles. a, A magnetic trapping field is generated by sinusoidal currents Itrap at trapping frequency Ωtrap, sent through two concentric conductive tracks (gold) on a chip (gray). A static magnetic field B0 is applied in the z-direction, as defined by the coordinate frame shown, also defining various modes of rotation. b, A ferromagnetic particle with magnetic moment µ is levitated in a pocket of the chip, with a (transparent) cover preventing escape during initial trapping. c, The rotating saddle potential caused by the alternating magnetic field results in a effective pseudo-potential UCOM for the...

FIG. 2. Experimental implementation and tunable particle dynamics. a, The particle motion is measured in a vacuum chamber by collecting back-scattered laser light on an avalanche photodiode (APD) via an objective and 90/10 beam splitter. A coil on top of the chamber provides the static magnetic field B0. b, The chip is mounted on a printed circuit board for electrical interfacing and enclosed with a cover glass. c Video still of the particle levitating in the trap. Note that the particle levitates in the center of the current loops, which is slightly off-center in the pocket. d, Scanning micrograph of the chip with current tracks. The blind hole is produced by laser ablation. The scale bar...

FIG. 3. Laser-induced particle thermodynamics. a- b, Translational eigenmode ωz (a) and librational eigenmode ωlib (b) as a function of laser power Plaser irradiated on the particle for different values of Pgas. The eigenfrequencies are extracted by fitting a Lorentzian to the PSDs. The fit of the eigenfrequencies is based on the thermodynamical model described in VI with the absorptivity α and remanent field Bsat(T0) as fitting parameters. c, Simulation of the change in remanent field Bsat with respect to its initial value at room temperature T0, as a function of gas pressure Pgas and laser power Plaser. For the simulation the model in VI is used with the fit value of absorptivity α from...

FIG. 4. Mechanical quality factor as function of gas pressure. a, Power spectral density of the ωx mode for dif- ferent values of gas pressure Pgas, vertically offset for clar- ity. Lines are a Lorentzian lineshape fit to the data from which we extract the linewidth. For decreasing gas pressure, the linewidth and eigenfrequency both decrease. b, Q-factor as a function of gas pressure Pgas for the translational ωx mode (circle) and librational ωlib mode (triangle). All qual- ity factors are determined from the Lorentzian linewidth. The dashed (dot-dashed) line is the expected Q-factor of the trans- lational (librational) mode for a spherical particle limited only by gas damping (see VII...

FIG. 5. Prospects for coupling the libration mechanical mode to a solid-state spin qubit. (a) The top glass of the sample is replaced by diamond containing nitrogen-vacancy defect spins. The dipole-dipole interaction parametrically couples the particle libration and spin state. The spin is placed at a distance |r| = a + d from the center of the ferromagnet. (b) Influence of the external magnetic field B0 on the librational eigenfrequency ωlib (top panel), and the single spin coupling rate g0 and mechanical heating rate Γlib (bottom panel).
